Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic. I'd tell myself I was going to study for like 34 hours a week, and sometimes I'd sit at my desk for that long, but a lot of it was just... existing near my materials rather than actually engaging. I'm a visual learner, so I'd have videos playing and diagrams open, but my mind would drift. I'd organize things in bursts when anxiety hit, then let it all fall apart again.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place. I'd skip classes for weeks—my attendance was terrible, I know—and then suddenly panic when I realized how behind I was. Deadlines barely motivated me because by the time I noticed them, it often felt too late anyway. Some weeks I'd do nothing, other weeks I'd be logged in constantly but not really accomplishing much.
Q3: Usually fear, if I'm being real. I'd get this scared feeling in my chest thinking about how much I was missing, and that would push me to log in. Sometimes it was just to check if I'd missed something catastrophic. Other times I'd log in because I was bored and the LMS felt like I was at least doing something productive, even if I wasn't really.
Q4: There were brief periods where I'd check in daily, usually when I was doing the mindfulness exercises they recommended and feeling more centered. But it never stuck for long. The routine would fall apart whenever something in my life shifted or I got overwhelmed. I'd log in frequently but it was more compulsive than habitual.
Q5: The videos were genuinely helpful for me since I learn visually. I watched over two hours worth of content because I could pause and rewind when my attention drifted. Discussion posts were also useful because seeing other students' perspectives helped things click. The peer interactions kept me feeling somewhat connected even when I wasn't showing up to class.
Q6: The readings and text-heavy resources I basically ignored. My brain just doesn't process walls of text well, especially when I'm anxious. I'd open them, scroll through, and close them without absorbing anything. The assignments—yeah, I barely completed any. I'd look at them and feel this wave of dread and just... close the tab.
Q7: If something felt achievable and visually engaging, I'd actually do it. Quick discussion posts where I could share a thought felt worth my time. But anything that required sustained concentration or felt like it would take hours? I'd convince myself it wasn't worth starting. The payoff had to feel immediate or I'd bail.
Q8: Optional stuff actually felt less threatening to me, weirdly. Required activities came with this pressure that made me freeze up. Optional things I could approach without the fear of failure attached. But then I'd spend time on optional discussions while ignoring the required assignments that actually mattered for my grade.
Q9: Yeah, definitely. I'd download lecture slides and videos to my tablet and study on the couch or in bed. I have good resources—nice laptop, tablet, reliable internet—so I could access things offline easily. I'd also use YouTube videos on the same topics because sometimes outside explanations clicked better than the course materials.
Q10: There were whole evenings where I'd be reviewing downloaded slides, rewatching videos I'd saved, or going through notes I'd made in my notebook. None of that showed up anywhere. I'd also talk through concepts with a friend who was in a similar program—that helped a lot but obviously left no digital trace.
Q11: All the time. I'd download a video, watch it once on the LMS so it registered, then watch it three more times offline while doing the mindfulness breathing they taught us. The LMS would show one view, but I'd have actually engaged with it multiple times. Same with slides—download once, reference constantly.
Q12: Mid-semester was brutal. I was scared most of the time—scared of falling behind, scared of failing, scared of being 27 and still struggling with basic discipline. When the anxiety spiked, I'd freeze instead of act. The mindfulness helped a bit, but there were weeks where I couldn't bring myself to engage at all.
Q13: Fatigue was huge. I'd stay up late anxiously scrolling through course materials without actually studying, then be too exhausted to attend class or focus the next day. Competing responsibilities pulled at me too—I have extracurriculars and life stuff that felt more manageable than coursework. The LMS became something I'd open when exhausted and just... stare at.
Q14: Constantly. I'd have the LMS open for hours, clicking through things, watching videos at 2x speed without absorbing anything. I was going through the motions because it felt like I should be doing something. But meaningful engagement? That was rare. I was present but not really there, if that makes sense.
Q15: The assignments. God, the assignments. I'd know they were important, see them sitting there, and feel this paralysis. It wasn't laziness—it was fear. Fear of doing them wrong, fear of how much work they'd take, fear of confirming I couldn't handle this. So I'd avoid them until it was too late, completing barely any.
Q16: Deadlines would cause these panic spikes where I'd suddenly log in constantly, trying to salvage what I could. But usually by then I'd already missed too much. The deadline would pass and I'd feel this mix of relief and shame. Then I'd disengage again until the next deadline panic. It was a terrible cycle.
Q17: When I actually got feedback, it affected me more than I expected. Good feedback would motivate me briefly. But I submitted so little work that feedback was rare. The lack of feedback made me feel invisible, which made it easier to drift further. It's like—if no one notices I'm failing, am I really failing?
Q18: The structure was pretty flexible, which was both good and bad for someone like me. Good because I could work at my own pace when I was functional. Bad because without rigid structure, I'd let everything slide. I needed more guardrails than the course provided, but that's on me, not the design.
Q19: The video content made engagement easier—visual stuff works for my brain. The discussion forums were accessible too. But the assignment structure was intimidating. Big projects with distant deadlines are my kryptonite. I needed smaller, more frequent checkpoints to stay on track. The openness of it all let me drift too easily.
Q20: That's literally me. I was highly active—logging in constantly, watching videos, posting in discussions, interacting with peers—but my assignment completion was abysmal. I think it's because engaging with content feels safe, but submitting work feels like being judged. The LMS activity was me circling the actual work without doing it.
Q21: I can see that happening for people who study efficiently offline. They download what they need, do the work on their own, and only log in to submit. That's not really me though—I had the opposite problem. But I respect people who can learn without needing to be constantly online. Sometimes less logging in means more actual focus.
Q22: Logs can show patterns—when you're active, what you click on, how long you spend. But they can't show if you're actually absorbing anything or just anxiously clicking around. They miss offline study entirely. They miss the emotional state behind the behavior. My high engagement numbers hide how scared and unfocused I actually was.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about and connecting with the material—not just showing up, but being mentally present. It means doing the work, not just consuming content. By that definition, I wasn't really engaged even though I was constantly around. True engagement would have meant completing assignments and actually learning, not just logging in.
Q24: The fear. The LMS can't show how scared I was every time I logged in, or how that fear paralyzed me instead of motivating me. It can't show the mindfulness exercises I did trying to calm down enough to focus. It can't show the offline studying or the conversations with friends. And it definitely can't show how much I actually wanted to succeed but couldn't get out of my own way.
